speaker: interviewer
text: OK. When we left off-- and I wanted to bring that back up again-- you mentioned that, you know, the first couple days, people thought, oh, this is great. We're going to go to work. We're getting extra food finally. How did they find out, though, that this is not what was going on? How did word get back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they used to-- like I said, they used to load them in the freight cars without food, without water, 80 to 100 people in a-- in a car. And-- but the-- the workers, the railroad workers, were Poles. And the train would go through Treblinka and then come back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the Polish engineers, the conductors, knew what's going on, because the smell around Treblinka was unbelievable. There were fires going day and night, 24 hours a day. And-- and people find-- found out about them, because some of the-- of the Poles working for them-- for the railroads told the stories, told-- told the-- the pictures they saw, the situation. But how did word come back to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were in contact with-- with the underground, and the Polish underground was collaborating with the Jewish underground. And they all exchanged information.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what sort of things would people do to resist? You said so then when people realized what was happening.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they started to resist by first, don't volunteer. Don't-- telling the people not to volunteer, not to give in to the Germans. And then they concentrating-- concentrated on ways to resist. And so they had to buy weapons. And they needed money for it. So they collected money from where we're headed. And they tried to buy weapons, which was very, very hard. Because like I said, in the beginning, the Poles were not helping the Jews at all. They didn't help them. And it was hard for the underground to buy a weapon, to buy guns and ammunition.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you part of the underground at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was, in a very small part, I was part of the underground. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How so?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- we used to break them, the-- the-- the walls, and make passages from one building to the other. And this you could do and not do. Show-- not to tell what we were doing, so we had to do it on the-- on the top floors, or on the attics, from one house-- this-- this served a double purpose. One was if they came in, the Germans, and they surrounded the building, and they told everybody to come down, and then march them out, so they could escape from one building to the other. So this-- this was-- this was the part I was doing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did that save-- did you have to do that yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My future wife then was living a couple of buildings away. And I would like to come to see her. So we had to break through. We have to go to the-- to the attics. And from there, go through from one to the other, until we reached her-- her building.

speaker: interviewer
text: When-- when the ghetto was being formed, and-- and-- what were the Polish citizens doing, the non-Jews? What sort of-- I mean, you mentioned that they were no help, but what sort of-- I mean, did they see what was going on? What-- what sort of things would they do? Well, they knew-- they knew all right what was going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: But like I said, they didn't help any. They didn't do anything-- anything whatsoever. There were-- I said that everybody tries to rescue themselves. They try to get away. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] There were a lot of people, a lot of Jews, who had relations with Poles-- whether business, professional. And they tried to solicit them to-- to do something, to help, at least to help their friends. And some of them did. Some of them helped. As a matter of fact, my wife and I survived by being hidden from the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: This happened-- my wife's sister had connections. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And she was given names and addresses of people who might help. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So through them, she was able to buy an apartment, or rent an apartment, which was part of the ghetto, until they made it smaller, until they-- Every time they took 10-- or 20,000 people out of the ghetto, they closed a street and incorporated that in the rest of the-- of the-- of the-- of city. So they rented an apartment, which was formerly-- formerly, a few weeks before, part of the ghetto. But not anymore. And she brought 19 people into this apartment to hide. Unfortunately, after about four-- four to five weeks, somebody uncovered, and the next thing was they sent a blackmail with a German soldier to this apartment and knocked on the door. And when my sister-in-law and the fellow who was supposedly her husband-- they both rented this apartment together-- opened the door, and they told them that they want 50,000 zloty. Otherwise, they'll go to the Germans and tell them. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So fortunately for us, we had a place to go that my sister-in-law had secured. We spent there three months in hiding. After three months, she told us to leave. She does not want to keep us anymore. And we're pleading with her that we have no place to go. Where can we go?

speaker: interviewee
text: She says she didn't care. Just, you just go. So we had to go someplace. And she found another place on the-- on the outskirts of the city. And we spent there almost two years in hiding.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who all was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Who-- how many of you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just-- just-- four of us. First, my wife and myself. Then her brother came. And then my sister came. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my father, who was also hidden but in a different part of the city, was supposed to come just before the Polish uprising. And he got trapped, and wasn't-- he couldn't move anymore. I tried to find out what happened to him, but there was no information. The building was destroyed. And I don't know what happened to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he part of the uprising?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never found out what happened to him?

words.text: found out what happened to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- your wife's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bala.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marcel.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were going from place to place, how did-- did you have to travel at night? I mean, obviously, you didn't have freedom of movement. How were you able to get around?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we left this apartment after the-- somebody denounced us, we had to go through the attics, because we were afraid that somebody might be there and watching our movements, so they can permanently blackmail us. So we went through. We came up out on a-- on a different street. I, of course, had my whole face bandaged, because I was afraid that I might be recognized as a Jew. The Poles had a wonderful gift of recognizing Jews. They can tell. You can be blond, and blue eyes, and speak a perfect Polish, and still-- that's what I learned from my landlady. She said to me when we came, you should not leave the apartment at all. Because despite of your Aryan look, your blue eyes and your blond hair, this was news to me, that the eyes-- they can recognize by the eyes, by the-- how you-- how you look.

speaker: interviewer
text: She was non-Jewish? And she warned you that she--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --they could tell. So how-- how would you manage?

speaker: interviewee
text: So I had covered my face, and I took a street car, and we went there. And we stayed there for-- for the duration.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't they ask you for papers as you were traveling?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. This was within the city.

speaker: interviewer
text: But I mean--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, just a street car. There was a special-- the-- the front of the street cars were reserved for Germans, the-- and the rear was for Poles. So I went with the-- with the Poles.

speaker: interviewer
text: But didn't you have like yellow stars on all your clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I took everything off, so I didn't leave those on. And my sister-in-law was in the same-- same train, the same street car. And she was always walking about 10, 12 feet ahead of me. And that's the way we came. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was your wife? You all came at different times?

speaker: interviewee
text: She came through. But she was-- she was-- we-- we didn't go together. We went separately. We went separately.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would you bring with you? I mean, if you knew--

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't have much. I had-- I remember I-- [LAUGHS] I had a pair of pajamas, one pair which was left, because we had all the stuff there in the-- in the former apartment. We didn't take anything with me, just what we could put on. I had three shirts, and-- and pajamas, and a-- one suit. And that was-- that's all I had.

speaker: interviewer
text: No money? No food? No--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: The landlady was buying the food. My sister-in-law would bring food. It-- it's so hard to explain. It's so hard for a person who didn't live through it to understand. It's even hard to visualize. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You had to be so careful with every move you made. If you went on there-- on there-- to the market, that's what our lady told us, you went to the market, and you bought a lot of products, there must be-- they could have been somebody who was watching you and following you. See, where is this person going with all this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this would lead to there-- there-- there were people whose profession, whose-- whose occupation, was to track down Jews, because they know that they can make an easy dollar by blackmailing them. So you had to be careful what you're buying. They had-- you had to be-- if you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let's see. We had-- we had rescued some watches from the store. In order to live, and pay the landlady, and buy food, we had to sell the-- the-- the watches. We didn't sell them all at once. So we sold one-- everything, my sister-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: She would go to a store and sell the watch. So the storekeepers and the Germans knew that the Jews are-- have to live, and they have to-- to sell whatever they had, whatever valuables they had. So they-- they was-- they was tracking the people down.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your sister-in-law's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh? My son's last name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Your sister-in-law.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, Bala. Mala, M-A-L-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And why was she the one going out and doing all this? How was she able to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she has very good looks. She looks, like I said, nobody could recognize her.

speaker: interviewer
text: She didn't look Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have false papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We all had false papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get those?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, through the underground.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they-- did they come in useful? I mean, were you using them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did your papers say?

speaker: interviewee
text: The papers just say the name, and address, and date of birth. And there was a seal, German governor.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you were a Christian, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure.

speaker: interviewer
text: Excuse me. What was your false name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Tadeusz Kostanecki.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did-- would you get that from an existing person? Or just--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, sometimes from a-- a dead person. Sometimes, you just thought of a name.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your sister-in-law was the one who was doing all this?

speaker: interviewee
text: She-- and-- and it's-- it's-- it's unbelievable. Before the war, she had a-- angina pectoris. It's a very bad heart condition. Before the war, she wouldn't-- she-- she shouldn't have bent down it was so bad. And here, she was carrying those big heads of cabbage and potatoes, which we ate a lot of. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she ever apprehended? Or was she ever close to being caught?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, but she-- she spent a few nights sitting on-- in strange courtyards, spending the night, because she didn't have a place to go. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how was she in contact with people that could help you? How did she know these people?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were, like I said, some Poles who tried to help. And one to the other, and she was very diligent, and she worked very hard to get to those people and to persuade them. As a matter of fact, this place, this last place where we spent two years, she visited this lady before and asked her, well, she has to give their accommodation. And she was refused, but not 100%. He says, oh, come back in three months, and we'll-- we'll see. It turned out that they had somebody hidden in the house, but they were lured out by the Germans, who through their agent, spread the rumor that if you go to this hotel, and you have $2,000-- and you have to have dollars. They wouldn't take any zlotys. For $2,000, you can go to there. You can stay in this hotel. And then we'll take you out into Switzerland. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So a lot of people did it. And they went straight into the gas chambers.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a set up?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure. So when she came back the second time after three months, those people were gone. So she was willing to take us in.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did she get rid of them? I mean, how-- not get rid-- I mean, how would-- what was her role in the-- in the set up, though?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, her-- she-- she didn't know anything about it. She was-- she was told by the people, we're going to go to this hotel, because they'll take us out and move us to Switzerland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, I see.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they-- they believed them. They trusted them. And-- and paid with their life for it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. I see. When you did go there, were you in contact with other people still in the ghetto? Did you try and get other people out at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: So once you left, you lost contact with--?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother and my sister were gone. The rest of my aunts and uncles were gone. There was no-- there was no family, nobody left. We didn't know of anybody who-- there was a-- the same mother who did so much for me and my wife, she had a father with her, an elderly man. And he got very sick while hidden. She took him to hospital, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and registered him under his false name, of course. And he died at the hospital. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they discovered that he is Jewish. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] They notified the director, the head of the hospital. Fortunately, he was a very gentle man, and he-- he didn't report it. He could have reported it. They buried him on a Christian cemetery. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And after the war, we exhumed the body and brought it to a Jewish cemetery. [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] And Mala was supposed to go to Australia after the war. And she got very sick. Her heart gave up, and she died on the way. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: This woman who helped you and your wife you made reference to-- what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mochska was a-- she was a-- she was a teacher, and her daughter was a librarian. She was divorced from her husband. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to this day, I'm trying to find out whether she did it out of good heart or for the money. But she was a very religious person. And I-- I tend to believe that she did it because she-- she thought it's-- it's the right thing to do, not the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was the one that kept you for the two years?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. I'm sorry. Was she the woman that-- that kept you for the two years, or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. But another very interesting story is that after the Russians-- the Russian army marched in, as we were-- we were freed in September of 1944. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that-- after the Russians, the Germans left, she said to us, now you're free. Go. I don't want you anymore. Because she got very-- a lot of criticism from her neighbors.

speaker: interviewer
text: For helping you?

speaker: interviewee
text: So she didn't-- she said, you're free. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you keep in contact with her after?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said you're still trying to find out-- does-- does she still have relatives alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: We corresponded with her for a few years. And later, somehow, I don't know. I don't know it's my fault or it's her fault, but we dropped the contact.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about the place that she had where you were hiding for two years. How were you able to hide for so long?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this was a-- used to be a beautiful villa, and the top two floors were demolished. Just the basement and the laundry room and the staff rooms, the-- was intact. So we had one room looking out to a beautiful, beautiful garden, with beautiful fruit trees. That's why we decided that-- that if we survive, we'd like to live in the country. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So when we came to the United States, we bought a chicken farm. [LAUGHS] And I had a hard time, because I didn't know where-- what came first, the chicken or the egg. And I didn't know anything about raising chickens.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you learned. Now, how were you able to-- to go back? All of-- you said it was difficult, obviously, to find food. Well, she had food growing there on the-- on the property there, or? You said, you know, if somebody went and bought a lot of food, it was obvious they were hiding people. How is-- how is it-- how was she able to keep all of you there for so long without ever being found out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it turned out that some of the neighbors knew about it, but they were good enough not to denounce us, not to threaten us or-- one day, I remember in the summer, I think it was in '44, in the summer. A-- somebody came in to buy flowers. She would cut flowers from her garden, and so on. And while she was cutting the flowers, this customer was looking around, walking around. And our-- the windows from our-- the window from our room was open, wide open. And me and my wife and I were standing against the wall, which was like this window here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we thought this-- this at least, this way here, she won't see us when she walks by. But she was very curious, and she looked in. She-- and I am-- I am positive that she saw us. There's no other way. She looked in, and we were right there. But she didn't make any-- she didn't make an issue out of it. She didn't say anything to her. She didn't do anything. But it was enough to keep us worried for a long time. Did you have like, a routine? If somebody came in, was there a place for you to hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was once-- like I mentioned before, they were very religious. They used to go to church every Sunday. And the divorced husband would come once in a while. This time, they left. We had a gate, which they have-- with a lock, which we would-- they would lock when they left. And then there were traps leading into the basement. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Stairs, I mean.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the door had a lock too. And one Sunday morning, we were sitting, and I don't know what we were doing. But then we heard a door closing. So I said to my wife, who's this? They just left for church. So I ran to the door, the front door, and locked it. Then I had to run through all the windows, because all the windows were open. And her husband knew about it. So he couldn't get in. I was always a step ahead of him, luckily. And-- so he sat down in the garden, and started a conversation with a neighbor, who was at the window. And guess what they talked about? The best thing Hitler could do is get rid of the Jews. And we listened to this here conversation, and we felt very, very bad about it. When finally they came home, he said to them, there's something funny going on here.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tried to get in, and I felt like the door was locked right in front of me. Then I went to the window, and-- and the windows were all locked. Step ahead of me. She said, oh-- she wouldn't admit to anything, of course. And something they [? wish ?] [? the ?] [? thing ?] up. But when they start talking about Hitler, what Hitler did good, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we didn't like it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Now, there were four of you staying there, or just the two of you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, at the beginning, there were the two of us. And then my-- my brother-in-law came, and my-- my sister came.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where was Mala? Where was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Mala was the courier. She was alone. You see, there were-- there was a Polish government in exile in London. And there was a government inside, inside Poland. They-- they both have-- had armies. They both had an underground. The one sponsored by the government in London was legitimate government, Polish government which escaped from Poland to England. And they helped the Jews. They even dropped money to help people who didn't have anything. So they had the list of people whom they supported. The other-- the other group had in their manifestor, I should say, that after the war, there would be no room for Jews in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm sorry, we need to stop taping. We're gonna pick up.
